name: NetSuite Finance Demo: Standard Reporting and Drilldown

avoid: custom saved reports for prospect demos
deep links unless used as fallback after a learned route
navigationPolicy.reportPolicy: Use standard NetSuite reports for prospect-facing demos unless the user explicitly asks for a custom report.

login.instructions: Open the browser window, sign in to NetSuite, and leave it on any authenticated page. The runner waits for the global Search box before starting.

demoRequest.topic: Generate a finance demo from the pre-demo notes. Focus on standard income statement and profitability reporting, drilldown from summary to transaction detail, export options and fewer spreadsheet handoffs and subsidiary and consolidated finance views. Demo scope: Financials first Services premium, including core financials, multi-entity reporting, approvals, SuiteProjects for flight or booking profitability, and Fixed Asset Management..
demoRequest.demoScope: Financials first Services premium, including core financials, multi-entity reporting, approvals, SuiteProjects for flight or booking profitability, and Fixed Asset Management.
demoRequest.competition: Known competition/status quo: SAP S/4HANA and Microsoft Dynamics are being considered alongside the current architecture of Access ERP, Jedox consolidation/planning, spreadsheets, email-heavy AP, and Payhawk/Access Expense in parts of the business.
demoRequest.manifestDemoModeInstruction: Turn the manifest into a customer-centered story. Use the selected audience and target audience playbook to personalize narration, proof moments, and the SC runbook.
demoRequest.demoStrategyInstruction: Demo strategy: Standard Platform Demo. Keep the tone polished, structured, and business-focused, pacing balanced and time-aware, with high-value workflows first, technical depth light to moderate unless requested, and story style broad end-to-end platform story connecting multiple stakeholder perspectives. Prioritize end-to-end business flows, cross-functional workflows, executive dashboards, reporting and analytics and automation examples. Behavior rules: prioritize breadth over deep specialization, ensure all major departments are represented, maintain consistent pacing, avoid excessive technical depth unless requested and make executive-level value visible throughout. Adapt the examples to General Business using finance visibility, operational control and growth readiness.
demoRequest.industryInstruction: Industry playbook: General Business. Use terminology such as finance visibility, operational control and growth readiness, emphasize KPIs such as cash position, profitability, close speed and forecast accuracy, and avoid industry-specific claims without discovery and unsupported benchmark statements.
demoRequest.outputLanguageInstruction: Write SC-facing output, setup guidance, and narrator talk tracks in English.
demoRequest.audienceInstruction: Demo to the Prospect audience type in a Mid-Market target audience context. Potential buyers evaluating whether the solution solves their business challenges and delivers measurable value. Focus on business value, ROI, ease of adoption and competitive differentiation. Include business pain points, before/after scenarios, success stories and metrics and outcomes. Avoid deep technical configuration, feature dumping, internal terminology and long setup explanations. For Mid-Market, use a process-oriented, business-value focused and real-world operational scenarios demo style. Prioritize process efficiency, visibility, integration and cross-team collaboration; include role-based workflows, analytics dashboards, integrations and approval processes; avoid oversimplified startup messaging, feature dumping, extremely technical implementation details and unnecessary enterprise governance complexity.
demoRequest.instructions:
Lead with the customer's business problem and desired outcome before showing screens.
Open with the biggest business hitters first: executive visibility, trusted numbers, consolidation confidence, close speed, project profitability, auditability, approvals, and fewer spreadsheets.
Use a crisp tell-show-tell rhythm: frame the point, show the shortest proof path, then land why it matters in plain language.
Use NetSuite global search and the navigation bar as the primary way to move through the product.
Use standard NetSuite reports for prospect-facing finance demos unless the audience explicitly asks for custom reporting.
Choose the optimal demo flow and put the highest-impact moments first.
Start with a general executive overview of NetSuite before moving into the specific workflow.
Keep SuiteProjects and Fixed Asset Management within the agreed scope, but do not let them overshadow the primary financials story.
Avoid feature tours, menu wandering, slow setup, dead clicks, and deep configuration unless intentionally requested.
Never announce internal labels such as value statement during narration.

additionalDemoSources.label: Use Standard NetSuite Reports First

additionalDemoSources.guidance: Use this as a routing and narration rule when the demo touches financial reporting, P&L, drilldown, export, or finance visibility.
additionalDemoSources.logic: If the demo asks for reporting or finance visibility, keep standard reports in the primary path and park custom reporting as an optional follow-up.

websiteContext.summary: Website was reachable at https://www.aircharterservice.com/, but no clear summary text was detected.
interesting_points: Website was scanned, but only limited business signals were detected from the homepage.
contradictions_or_checks: Pre-demo notes mention Veluxis, but the scanned website context does not. Confirm whether Veluxis should be part of this demo story or treated as a separate operating model.

codexPrepAnalysis.analysis:
# Codex Prep Operator Analysis
Task title: created by demo helper - aircharterservice.com

## Company Context That Should Shape The Demo
- ACS is a global aircraft charter broker, not an aircraft operator, spanning private jet, group, and cargo charter services.
- Public site signals scale: 42 offices, 700+ aviation experts, 47 languages, 35,000+ flights annually, and $1B+ 2025 gross transaction value.
- The business is highly multi-country and service-led: local offices, local customer requirements, local tax/e-invoicing pressures, and global procurement capability.
- Booking economics appear naturally “project-like”: each bespoke flight/booking needs revenue, supplier costs, route, aircraft type, broker/office attribution, and margin visibility.
- Public financials show subsidiaries across countries including UK/England, USA, Canada, Germany, Brazil, UAE/Dubai, Hong Kong, China, Spain, France, South Africa, India, Switzerland, Australia, Ireland, Kazakhstan, Singapore, Mexico, Thailand, and Italy.
- ACS also has localized websites for more countries than the statutory subsidiary list, so confirm which countries are active legal entities versus marketing/local-language presence.

## Likely ERP Priorities
- Consolidation confidence across many entities, currencies, local charts, local tax needs, and management reporting views.
- Executive visibility into trusted group numbers without depending on spreadsheet consolidation or external reporting workarounds.
- Faster close with drilldown from consolidated P&L to subsidiary, office, booking, customer, supplier bill, and journal detail.
- Flight/booking profitability using SuiteProjects as the lightweight profitability spine, not as a heavy resource-management demo.
- Clear approvals with supporting context, thresholds, budget holder visibility, and fewer “approve without knowing what this is” moments.
- AR quality and dunning support through centralized documents, transaction history, booking references, and credit note visibility.
- Integration fit with IBOS CRM, Jedox, Payhawk/Access Expense, ODBC/reporting needs, and third-party e-invoicing providers.
- Fixed asset control for office equipment, software/intangibles, straight-line depreciation, and audit-ready asset registers.

## Recommended Demo Direction
Open with the business problem: ACS is scaling internationally faster than a UK-centric ERP and spreadsheet-heavy processes can comfortably support. The desired outcome is trusted group finance: local compliance, consistent consolidation, booking-level profitability, and audit-ready approvals.

Start with a short executive NetSuite overview focused on one platform for subsidiaries, transactions, reporting, approvals, projects, and assets. Then move quickly into the highest-value proof path:

1. Executive dashboard and global search: show one place to land, search, and navigate without menu wandering.
2. Standard financial reporting: consolidated income statement, subsidiary view, local entity view, and drilldown to transactions.
3. Consolidation and close: demonstrate confidence in group numbers, currencies, eliminations/high-level close status if available, and fewer spreadsheet handoffs.
4. Booking profitability: use SuiteProjects to show a flight/booking with revenue, supplier costs, broker/office/location, aircraft type, route, and margin.
5. Approval workflow: show a supplier bill or expense approval with coding, booking reference, supporting documents, and threshold context.
6. AR/dunning context: show customer balance, invoice backup, booking reference, and why centralized information reduces payment delays.
7. Fixed assets: keep this short, showing acquisition, depreciation, and register/reporting.
8. Integration/reporting wrap: explain where IBOS, Jedox, Payhawk/Access Expense, e-invoicing provider, and ODBC/reporting needs fit.

Keep the rhythm crisp: frame the ACS issue, show the shortest NetSuite proof, land the business impact.

## Scope And Account Prep Implications
- Configure a multi-entity demo story with at least UK group/consolidation plus representative subsidiaries such as USA, UAE/Dubai, Spain, India, Germany or France, and Singapore/Canada.
- Prepare dimensions for legal entity, office/department, business type such as Private, Group, Cargo, and possibly broker/salesperson.
- Create sample flight/booking projects with start date, flight date, route, aircraft type, customer, supplier/airline costs, broker attribution, and margin reporting.
- Use standard NetSuite reports first: income statement, balance sheet, trial balance, A/R aging, A/P aging, project profitability, fixed asset reports.
- Include examples of local reporting needs: local GAAP/chart mapping, tax reporting, e-invoicing status/provider touchpoint, and language-aware invoice templates.
- Prepare approval workflows with thresholds, budget holder approval, document attachments, and transaction context.
- Include fixed assets for office equipment and software/intangibles with straight-line depreciation.
- Have integration talking points ready for IBOS booking references, Jedox planning/reporting, Payhawk/Access Expense, third-party e-invoicing, and ODBC/export expectations.

## Discovery Gaps That Could Hurt The Demo
- Confirm the active legal entities/countries in scope for phase 1 versus future rollout.
- Confirm which countries require local GAAP books, statutory chart of accounts, local tax reporting, language requirements, and e-invoicing.
- Confirm whether Multi-Book Accounting is required now or only a future requirement.
- Clarify current consolidation pain: what stays in Jedox, what NetSuite should replace, and what management reporting still needs to feed FP&A.
- Confirm IBOS integration scope: booking reference only, customers, invoices, credit notes, project/flight metadata, or full transaction lifecycle.
- Clarify expenses strategy: Payhawk user count, Access Expense usage, paperwork users, and whether expenses are phase 1 or procure-to-pay phase 2.
- Confirm approval matrix by transaction type, entity, amount threshold, budget holder, and whether approvers need project margin visibility.
- Confirm Voluxis/private aircraft management accounting treatment: balance sheet accounts, owner disbursements, recharge logic, and reporting needs.

Sources used: ACS home page, global network, ACS websites page, financials page, 2025 annual report, private/group/cargo charter pages.

context.preDemoNotes:
procure 2 pay phase2? how many users for expenses payhawk?
budget holders, approvals etc? / FP&A / advanced inventory?
multibook ACS / different roles AR/AP go in specifically

Paul miles project manager 
Henry Davis business analyst
Rob IT director – tech side
Jo head of IT operations – responsible for production
Jonathan financial controller
Tom Finance transformation manager (previous financial controller)
Cary – group CFO

They looked in a separate procure to pay project but started looking in entire ERP project
Multiple countries / consolidations 

Payhawk is being used for one part of business for expenses (not for core air charter) they use access expense or old school paperwork.

Biggest challenges are grouping, consolidations so far it’s been very UK focused with the ERP system they currently have. 

Local GAAP chart of accounts requirement for instance 
Tax reporting in local requirements 
E-invoicing has been more of an issue

Other challenges are expansion, every company etc is a new database now 

Integrations are challenges as well, how it can integrate with existing architecture 

Consolidation is being done in Jedox which works well (NetSuite can do this way better) this must be external because of access ERP system

We need a list of countries they have as active countries.
Some countries require languages even 2 languages in the same country 

Tracking projects for each flights, broker who sold the flight who helped them out, start date end date, route, aircraft type, associated transactions who did we sold to, what costs etc that is all linked to the project.

aircraft owner / broker takes care of appreciation/inventory etc (rest will be charged to them)

private jet management company (veluxis) wealthy company/individuals owned aircrafts. Veluxis manages them on their behalf – they arrange maintenance and flights to happen. They sell them as charters but they operate slightly different then the core business: we’re arranging costs we’re incurring them and then we dispursing them to the aircraft ower, currently that is being done with different balance sheet accounts. 

They don’t operate with PO’s atm, each booking is a bespoke flight (project) the brokers will be sending the invoices from the airlines. Email heavy process making sure payment is being done, maybe credit open etc heavy email process. 

Dunning requirements – they can report quite effectively. The delay in payment from customers is usually not having the correct data send to them, no centralized storage. 

IBOS CRM currently in place, maybe a future scope to replace this – focus is on finance side . Just bring in booking reference etc, maybe credit notes 

projects do not require resources right now but might be something in the future (related to DDI? Customers need to know statuses etc)

Revenue is being split by location and type of business (cargo flights, private jets, passenger planes etc) Sometimes they recognize revenue related to dubai and also something related to the Indian office – need to know which legal entity and which office/department is it linked to.

Recognition takes place on the date/month when the flight takes place.

Planning and begetting / FP&A is taking place in jedox system especially different budgets. Done in pounds world-wide. It’s not written back in the dimensions in the ERP system.

FP&A phase 2 is being done in spreadsheets atm, but it would be nice to move on eventually.

deferring of expenses no, just for bills

fixed assets – office equipment / intangible assets – currently spreadsheets is straight line.

ODBC requirements although a lot of reports seem to be out of the box here so we need to highlight this during the demo. 

Sending out templates, need to show template configuration

E-invoicing submissions are being done with a third party, some places might make sense to do it themselves but other places they like someone else doing it. Sending/Receiving. 


Question – which countries are you sending E-invoices to and/or are receiving them

approval matrix in place with thresholds etc, but people don’t know what they are approving.

demoStrategy.description: A broad, structured product demonstration that showcases the overall platform, major capabilities, and end-to-end business value within a fixed timeframe.
demoStrategy.pacing: balanced and time-aware, with high-value workflows first

setupPlan.accountCreateWarning: Creating records or configuration in NetSuite can affect the connected account. Always confirm the exact account, role, and records before executing.

items.reason: needed if operational transactions require item detail

items.reason: needed to demonstrate multi-entity filtering or consolidation

items.label: fixed assets demo context
items.reason: needed if fixed assets are in scope for the personalized story or setup prompt

items.label: planning and budgeting demo context
items.reason: needed if FP&A, budgets, or planning are in scope for the demo

items.label: services SKU or project demo context
items.reason: needed if the scoped demo should show services-led financials or project profitability

slider.label: Balanced
slider.description: Add value points on each major page or section.

slider.label: Heavy
slider.description: Add value points throughout the demo.

runButtons.label: Dry Run
runButtons.description: Print the planned demo without opening NetSuite.

runButtons.label: Rehearse And Prep Account
runButtons.description: Create the account prep buffer, then run the browser without audio, verify pages, save timings and screenshots.

runButtons.label: Run Live Demo
runButtons.description: Run the browser automation with narration.

segments.title: Frame The Executive NetSuite Overview
segments.objective: Set up the general NetSuite platform story before opening detailed finance workflows.
segments.valueStatement: aircharterservice.com first sees why NetSuite matters at the executive level: visibility, control, trusted numbers, and a clear path from summary to action.
segments.narration: Before we open the first report, frame the executive view for aircharterservice.com: NetSuite is the operating system where finance can see performance, prove the detail, and make decisions from one controlled place. We will start with the highest-value proof moments, then move into supporting detail. Keep the live path inside this scope: Financials first Services premium, including core financials, multi-entity reporting, approvals, SuiteProjects for flight or booking profitability, and Fixed Asset Management..

actions.text: Executive overview. Audience: Prospect. Target: Mid-Market. Strategy: Standard Platform Demo. Industry: General Business. Stay inside this demo scope unless the SC deliberately branches: Financials first Services premium, including core financials, multi-entity reporting, approvals, SuiteProjects for flight or booking profitability, and Fixed Asset Management..

segments.title: Open The Standard Income Statement
segments.objective: Use NetSuite search/navigation to open a standard profitability report.
segments.valueStatement: aircharterservice.com gets a fast, trusted performance view that connects directly to trusted reporting and drilldown from summary to detail.
segments.narration: For Prospect, keep the narration persuasive, outcome-oriented and story-driven and anchored in business value, ROI, ease of adoption and competitive differentiation. For aircharterservice.com, let's start where a finance leader usually wants to start: the standard income statement. This gives a quick view of revenue, margin, overheads, and net profit from one controlled place in NetSuite.

segments.title: Read The Finance Story
segments.objective: Show the CFO-level view: sales, gross profit, operating profit, and net result.
segments.valueStatement: A first-time viewer can understand performance without needing to know where every transaction lives.
segments.narration: For someone new to the system, this report is intentionally familiar. Turnover is at the top, overheads sit below it, and operating profit and net profit are easy to find at the bottom.

segments.title: Show Report Filters
segments.objective: Highlight the filter set finance can use to re-slice the result.
segments.valueStatement: aircharterservice.com can re-run the same standard report by period, subsidiary, and accounting book, which supports trusted reporting and drilldown from summary to detail.
segments.narration: Now let's look at how a team can change the lens. In the standard report, the most important controls are period, date range, subsidiary context, and accounting book.

segments.title: Drill Into The Numbers
segments.objective: Move from summary P&L to the transaction-level support behind a number.
segments.valueStatement: When a number is questioned, the team can investigate it from the report instead of starting a separate reconciliation exercise.
segments.narration: If someone asks what sits behind a number, we can drill from the summary into the supporting detail. That turns the report from a static total into an explainable result.
fallback: If the drilldown route changes, click a blue linked report amount or account line such as Revenue, then return to the saved report.

segments.title: Show Export Options
segments.objective: Show how the report can be shared or taken offline.
segments.valueStatement: Teams can keep NetSuite as the source of truth while still producing the files people expect for reviews, audits, and board packs.
segments.narration: When the report needs to leave the system, the export options are already here. Excel, PDF, CSV, Word, and print are available without rebuilding the report somewhere else.
